title: The Path to the Supreme
introduction: Chapter 8 of the Bhagavad Gita, Akshara Brahma Yoga (The Yoga of the Imperishable Brahman), is one of the most concise yet profound chapters on the nature of death, the afterlife, liberation, and how to reach the Supreme at the moment of departure. Arjuna asks seven direct, existential questions: What is Brahman? What is adhyatma? What is karma? What is adhibhuta? What is adhidaiva? What is adhiyajna? And — most importantly — how can one remember You (Krishna) at the time of death? Krishna answers each clearly, then reveals the two paths after death (the path of light leading to no return, and the path of darkness leading to return), explains how constant remembrance ensures one reaches Him, and ends with the famous promise: whoever remembers Me at death attains Me. For modern readers — especially those wrestling with mortality anxiety, fear of the unknown, legacy concerns, or the question 'what happens when we die?' — this chapter is direct medicine. In an age of medical prolonging of life, sudden losses, existential podcasts, and near-death experience stories, Krishna offers certainty: your state of consciousness at death determines your destination, and loving remembrance of the Divine is the safest passage. The essence remains intact: the imperishable Brahman is the ultimate goal, and single-minded devotion to the Supreme Person is the easiest way to reach that goal even amid the chaos of life and the mystery of death.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अर्जुन उवाच |
किं तद्ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म पुरुषोत्तम |
अधिभूतं च किं प्रोक्तमधिदैवं किमुच्यते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca
kiṁ tad brahma kim adhyātmaṁ kiṁ karma puruṣottama
adhibhūtaṁ ca kiṁ proktam adhidaivaṁ kim ucyate
shlokas.translation: Arjuna inquired: O my Lord, O Supreme Person, what is Brahman? What is the self? What are fruitive activities? What is this material manifestation? And what are the demigods? Please explain this to me.
shlokas.millennial: Arjuna asks Krishna to explain the meaning of Brahman, the self, action, the material world, and the demigods. This verse opens the chapter on the path to the supreme by showing Arjuna's sincere desire for clear knowledge. For people in their thirties who seek understanding amid complex life questions this verse reflects the search for foundational truths. For example a professional reflecting on career purpose and deeper meaning asks similar questions about the soul and the world. Detailed meaning: Arjuna's questions cover the essential concepts of transcendental knowledge including the absolute reality the individual soul action the physical universe and celestial beings. This sets the stage for Krishna to reveal how to reach the supreme. The verse makes readers eager to receive the answers. Practice: ask sincere questions about the nature of reality and the self so your understanding of the path to the supreme grows clear and practical.
shlokas.genz: Arjuna asks Krishna to explain what Brahman the self action the material world and the demigods really mean. This verse opens the chapter on the path to the supreme by showing Arjuna's genuine curiosity. For young adults navigating big life questions this verse captures the vibe of seeking real answers. For example someone scrolling through philosophy or self help wondering about the soul and reality asks the same things. Detailed meaning: Arjuna's questions cover the core ideas of transcendental knowledge including the absolute the individual action the physical realm and higher beings. This prepares for Krishna to show the way to the supreme. The verse makes you eager for the answers. Practice: ask honest questions about the nature of reality and the self so your path to the supreme becomes clear and real.
shlokas.genalpha: Arjuna asks Krishna to explain what Brahman the self action the material world and the demigods really mean. This verse opens the chapter on the path to the supreme by showing Arjuna's curious heart. For children this is like asking a big friend about the big truth the inside self the play the big world and the shining friends. Detailed meaning: Arjuna's questions cover the important ideas of transcendental knowledge including the absolute the individual action the physical realm and higher beings. This prepares for Krishna to show the way to the supreme. The verse makes you curious for the answers. Practice: ask gentle questions about the nature of truth and the self so your path to the supreme becomes clear and fun.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अधियज्ञः कथं कोऽत्र देहे अस्मिन्मधुसूदन |
प्रयाणकाले च कथं ज्ञेयोऽसि नियतात्मभिः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
adhiyajñaḥ kathaṁ ko ’tra dehe ’smin madhusūdana
prayāṇa-kāle ca kathaṁ jñeyo ’si niyatātmabhiḥ
shlokas.translation: How does the Lord of sacrifice live in the body, O Madhusūdana? And how can those engaged in devotional service know You at the time of death?
shlokas.millennial: Arjuna asks how the Lord of sacrifice resides in the body and how those with controlled minds can know the divine at the time of death. This verse continues Arjuna's questions about the supreme. For people in their thirties facing life's transitions this verse addresses the ultimate moment. For example someone preparing for major life changes wonders how to remember the divine at critical times. Detailed meaning: Arjuna inquires about the presence of the divine in the body and the way to remember Him at the final moment. This shows the practical side of transcendental knowledge. The verse makes readers eager for the guidance. Practice: keep the divine in mind during daily life so remembrance comes naturally at important moments.
shlokas.genz: Arjuna asks how the Lord of sacrifice lives in the body and how those with steady minds can know the divine at the time of death. This verse continues Arjuna's questions about the supreme. For young adults thinking about big transitions this verse addresses the ultimate moment. For example someone facing change wonders how to stay connected to higher energy at key times. Detailed meaning: Arjuna inquires about the presence of the divine in the body and the way to remember Him at the final moment. This shows the practical side of transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you eager for the guidance. Practice: keep the divine in mind during daily life so remembrance comes naturally at important moments.
shlokas.genalpha: Arjuna asks how the Lord of sacrifice lives in the body and how those with steady minds can know the divine at the time of death. This verse continues Arjuna's questions about the supreme. For children this is like asking how the big friend stays inside and how to remember him at the end of a game. Detailed meaning: Arjuna inquires about the presence of the divine in the body and the way to remember Him at the final moment. This shows the practical side of transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you curious for the guidance. Practice: keep the big friend in mind during daily play so remembrance comes naturally at important moments.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमं स्वभावोऽध्यात्ममुच्यते |
भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञितः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
akṣaraṁ brahma paramaṁ svabhāvo ’dhyātmam ucyate
bhūta-bhāvodbhava-karo visargaḥ karma-saṁjñitaḥ
shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: The indestructible, transcendental living entity is called Brahman, and his eternal nature is called adhyātma, the self. Action pertaining to the development of the material bodies of the living entities is called karma, or fruitive activities.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna defines Brahman as the indestructible transcendental living entity and adhyatma as the eternal self. Karma is the action that creates material bodies. This verse answers Arjuna's questions in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For people in their thirties seeking definitions this verse provides clarity. For example a student of life who learns these terms understands the soul action and the body better. Detailed meaning: Krishna gives precise definitions of Brahman the self and karma. This foundational knowledge is essential for wisdom. The verse makes readers eager for more explanations. Practice: reflect on these definitions so your understanding of the self and action becomes clear.
shlokas.genz: Krishna defines Brahman as the indestructible transcendental living entity and adhyatma as the eternal self. Karma is the action that creates material bodies. This verse answers Arjuna's questions in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For young adults seeking definitions this verse provides clarity. For example a learner who grasps these terms understands the soul action and the body better. Detailed meaning: Krishna gives precise definitions of Brahman the self and karma. This foundational knowledge is essential for wisdom. The verse makes you eager for more explanations. Practice: reflect on these definitions so your understanding of the self and action becomes clear.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna defines Brahman as the indestructible transcendental living entity and adhyatma as the eternal self. Karma is the action that creates material bodies. This verse answers Arjuna's questions in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For children this is like knowing the forever self the inside self and the play that makes bodies. Detailed meaning: Krishna gives precise definitions of Brahman the self and karma. This foundational knowledge is essential for wisdom. The verse makes you curious for more explanations. Practice: reflect on these definitions so your understanding of the self and play becomes clear.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः पुरुषश्चाधिदैवतम् |
अधियज्ञोऽहमेवात्र देहे देहभृतां वर ||
shlokas.transliteration:
adhibhūtaṁ kṣaro bhāvaḥ puruṣaś cādhidaivatam
adhiyajño ’ham evātra dehe deha-bhṛtāṁ vara
shlokas.translation: O best of the embodied beings, the physical nature, which is constantly changing, is called adhibhūta [the material manifestation]. The universal form of the Lord, which includes all the demigods, like those of the sun and moon, is called adhidaiva. And I, the Supreme Lord, represented as the Supersoul in the heart of every embodied being, am called adhiyajña [the Lord of sacrifice].
shlokas.millennial: Krishna defines adhibhuta as the changing physical nature adhidaiva as the universal form including demigods and Himself as adhiyajna the supersoul in every heart. This verse completes the answers. For people in their thirties understanding the world this verse gives the full picture. For example a thinker who sees the material world the cosmic form and the inner divine understands reality comprehensively. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the terms adhibhuta adhidaiva and adhiyajna. This knowledge integrates the material and spiritual. The verse makes readers eager to apply these concepts. Practice: see the changing world the cosmic order and the divine in your heart so your view of life becomes complete.
shlokas.genz: Krishna defines adhibhuta as the changing physical nature adhidaiva as the universal form including demigods and Himself as adhiyajna the supersoul in every heart. This verse completes the answers. For young adults understanding the world this verse gives the full picture. For example a thinker who sees the material world the cosmic form and the inner divine understands reality comprehensively. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the terms adhibhuta adhidaiva and adhiyajna. This knowledge integrates the material and spiritual. The verse makes you eager to apply these concepts. Practice: see the changing world the cosmic order and the divine in your heart so your view of life becomes complete.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna defines adhibhuta as the changing physical nature adhidaiva as the universal form including shining friends and Himself as adhiyajna the big friend in every heart. This verse completes the answers. For children this is like knowing the changing world the big sky form and the big friend inside. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the terms adhibhuta adhidaiva and adhiyajna. This knowledge integrates the material and spiritual. The verse makes you curious to apply these concepts. Practice: see the changing world the big sky order and the divine in your heart so your view of life becomes complete.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अन्तकाले च मामेव स्मरन्मुक्त्वा कलेवरम् |
यः प्रयाति स मद्भावं याति नास्त्यत्र संशयः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
antakāle ca mām eva smaran muktvā kalevaram
yaḥ prayāti sa mad-bhāvaṁ yāti nāsty atra saṁśayaḥ
shlokas.translation: And whoever, at the end of his life, quits his body remembering Me alone at once attains My nature. Of this there is no doubt.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that one who remembers Him alone at the time of death attains His nature without doubt. This verse shows the power of remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For people in their thirties reflecting on life's end this verse brings peace. For example someone who practices remembering the divine daily feels prepared for the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that final remembrance of the divine leads to union with Him. This is the practical application of wisdom. The verse makes readers eager to cultivate remembrance. Practice: remember the divine throughout life so at the end the remembrance comes naturally and leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that one who remembers Him alone at the time of death attains His nature without doubt. This verse shows the power of remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For young adults thinking about life's big moments this verse brings peace. For example someone who practices remembering higher energy daily feels prepared for the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that final remembrance of the divine leads to union with Him. This is the practical application of wisdom. The verse makes you eager to cultivate remembrance. Practice: remember the divine throughout life so at the end the remembrance comes naturally and leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna assures that one who remembers Him alone at the time of death attains His nature without doubt. This verse shows the power of remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For children this is like remembering the big friend at the end of the day and feeling safe. For example a child who thinks of the big friend always feels close. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that final remembrance of the divine leads to union with Him. This is the practical application of wisdom. The verse makes you curious to cultivate remembrance. Practice: remember the divine throughout life so at the end the remembrance comes naturally and leads to the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
यं यं वापि स्मरन्भावं त्यजत्यन्ते कलेवरम् |
तं तमेवैति कौन्तेय सदैव तद्भावभावितः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yaṁ yaṁ vāpi smaran bhāvaṁ tyajaty ante kalevaram
taṁ tam evaiti kaunteya sadā tad-bhāva-bhāvitaḥ
shlokas.translation: Whatever state of being one remembers when he quits his body, O son of Kuntī, to that state he will attain without fail.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that one attains the state remembered at death according to constant contemplation. This verse explains the law of final thought. For people in their thirties shaping their thoughts this verse brings responsibility. For example someone who cultivates positive divine thoughts daily ensures a peaceful transition. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the dominant thought at death determines the next state. This knowledge emphasizes lifelong practice. The verse makes readers eager to control their thoughts. Practice: fill your mind with divine remembrance daily so your final thought leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna states that one attains the state remembered at death according to constant contemplation. This verse explains the law of final thought. For young adults shaping their thoughts this verse brings responsibility. For example someone who cultivates positive divine thoughts daily ensures a peaceful transition. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the dominant thought at death determines the next state. This knowledge emphasizes lifelong practice. The verse makes you eager to control your thoughts. Practice: fill your mind with divine remembrance daily so your final thought leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna states that one attains the state remembered at death according to constant contemplation. This verse explains the law of final thought. For children this is like the last thought before sleep shapes the dream. For example a child who thinks of the big friend at bedtime has happy dreams. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the dominant thought at death determines the next state. This knowledge emphasizes lifelong practice. The verse makes you curious to control your thoughts. Practice: fill your mind with divine remembrance daily so your final thought leads to the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु मामनुस्मर युध्य च |
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिरमेवैष्यसि संशयम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu mām anusmara yudhya ca
mayy arpita-mano-buddhir mām evaiṣyasi saṁśayam
shlokas.translation: Therefore, Arjuna, you should always think of Me in the form of Kṛṣṇa and at the same time carry out your prescribed duty of fighting. With your activities dedicated to Me and your mind and intelligence fixed on Me, you will attain Me without doubt.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna advises Arjuna to remember Him always while performing duty and to dedicate mind and intellect to Him for certain attainment. This verse combines action and remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For people in their thirties balancing duties this verse is the perfect formula. For example a parent who remembers the divine while fulfilling family responsibilities attains the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that constant remembrance and dedicated action lead to union without doubt. This is the practical path. The verse makes readers eager to apply it. Practice: remember the divine in every moment while doing your duty so you attain the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna advises Arjuna to remember Him always while performing duty and to dedicate mind and intellect to Him for certain attainment. This verse combines action and remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For young adults balancing life this verse is the perfect formula. For example a creator who remembers the divine while creating attains the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that constant remembrance and dedicated action lead to union without doubt. This is the practical path. The verse makes you eager to apply it. Practice: remember the divine in every moment while doing your duty so you attain the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna advises Arjuna to remember Him always while performing duty and to dedicate mind and intellect to Him for certain attainment. This verse combines action and remembrance in the path of knowledge and wisdom. For children this is like remembering the big friend while playing and offering the play to Him. For example a child who thinks of the big friend during games feels close. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that constant remembrance and dedicated action lead to union without doubt. This is the practical path. The verse makes you curious to apply it. Practice: remember the divine in every moment while doing your duty so you attain the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा नान्यगामिना |
परमं पुरुषं दिव्यं याति पार्थानुचिन्तयन् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
abhyāsa-yoga-yuktena cetasā nānya-gāminā
paramaṁ puruṣaṁ divyaṁ yāti pārthānucintayan
shlokas.translation: He who meditates on Me as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, his mind constantly engaged in remembering Me, undeviated from the path, he, O Pārtha, is sure to reach Me.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that one who meditates on Him with unwavering mind reaches Him. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance. For people in their thirties building focus this verse is motivating. For example someone who keeps the divine in mind amid work reaches the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches unwavering meditation leads to the divine. This is the essence of the path. The verse makes readers eager to practice. Practice: keep your mind fixed on the divine without deviation so you reach the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that one who meditates on Him with unwavering mind reaches Him. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance. For young adults building focus this verse is motivating. For example someone who keeps the divine in mind amid work reaches the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches unwavering meditation leads to the divine. This is the essence of the path. The verse makes you eager to practice. Practice: keep your mind fixed on the divine without deviation so you reach the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna assures that one who meditates on Him with unwavering mind reaches Him. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance. For children this is like thinking of the big friend all the time. For example a child who keeps the big friend in mind reaches happy union. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches unwavering meditation leads to the divine. This is the essence of the path. The verse makes you curious to practice. Practice: keep your mind fixed on the divine without deviation so you reach the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
कविं पुराणमनुशासितारमणोरणीयांसमनुस्मरेद्यः |
सर्वस्य धातारमचिन्त्यरूपमादित्यवर्णं तमसः परस्तात् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
kaviṁ purāṇam anuśāsitāram aṇor aṇīyāṁsam anusmared yaḥ
sarvasya dhātāram acintya-rūpam āditya-varṇaṁ tamasaḥ parastāt
shlokas.translation: One should meditate upon the Supreme Person as the one who knows everything, as He who is the oldest, who is the controller, who is smaller than the smallest, who is the maintainer of everything, who is beyond all material conception, who is inconceivable, and who is always a person. He is luminous like the sun, and He is transcendental, beyond this material nature.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the supreme as the knower the oldest the controller smaller than the smallest the maintainer inconceivable luminous like the sun and transcendental. This verse gives the object of meditation. For people in their thirties contemplating the divine this verse provides attributes. For example a seeker who meditates on these qualities finds the supreme real. Detailed meaning: Krishna paints the attributes of the supreme for meditation. This helps focus the mind. The verse makes readers eager to meditate on these qualities. Practice: meditate on the supreme as described so your understanding deepens.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the supreme as the knower the oldest the controller smaller than the smallest the maintainer inconceivable luminous like the sun and transcendental. This verse gives the object of meditation. For young adults contemplating the divine this verse provides attributes. For example a seeker who meditates on these qualities finds the supreme real. Detailed meaning: Krishna paints the attributes of the supreme for meditation. This helps focus the mind. The verse makes you eager to meditate on these qualities. Practice: meditate on the supreme as described so your understanding deepens.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna describes the supreme as the knower the oldest the controller smaller than the smallest the maintainer inconceivable luminous like the sun and transcendental. This verse gives the object of meditation. For children this is like thinking of the big friend as the smartest the oldest the boss the tiniest the helper the unimaginable the bright and beyond everything. Detailed meaning: Krishna paints the attributes of the supreme for meditation. This helps focus the mind. The verse makes you curious to meditate on these qualities. Practice: meditate on the supreme as described so your understanding deepens.

shlokas.sanskrit:
प्रयाणकाले मनसाचलेन भक्त्या युक्तो योगबलेन चैव |
भ्रुवोर्मध्ये प्राणमावेश्य सम्यक् स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
prayāṇa-kāle manasācalena bhaktyā yukto yoga-balena caiva
bhruvor madhye prāṇam āveśya samyak sa taṁ paraṁ puruṣam upaiti divyam
shlokas.translation: One who, at the time of death, fixes his life air between the eyebrows and, by the strength of yoga, with an undeviating mind, engages himself in remembering the Supreme Lord in full devotion, will certainly attain to the Supreme Personality of Godhead.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that one who at death fixes the life air between the eyebrows with yoga strength undeviating mind and full devotion attains the supreme. This verse shows the method at death. For people in their thirties preparing for the end this verse brings guidance. For example someone who practices yoga and devotion daily is prepared for the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the technique of fixing the life force and remembering the divine at death. This leads to the supreme. The verse makes readers eager to prepare. Practice: practice yoga and devotion daily so at the final moment you remember the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that one who at death fixes the life air between the eyebrows with yoga strength undeviating mind and full devotion attains the supreme. This verse shows the method at death. For young adults thinking about life's end this verse brings guidance. For example someone who practices yoga and devotion daily is prepared for the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the technique of fixing the life force and remembering the divine at death. This leads to the supreme. The verse makes you eager to prepare. Practice: practice yoga and devotion daily so at the final moment you remember the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna assures that one who at death fixes the life air between the eyebrows with yoga strength undeviating mind and full devotion attains the supreme. This verse shows the method at death. For children this is like remembering the big friend at the end with calm heart. For example a child who practices calm and love is ready. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the technique of fixing the life force and remembering the divine at death. This leads to the supreme. The verse makes you curious to prepare. Practice: practice yoga and devotion daily so at the final moment you remember the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit: ओं इत्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन् | यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम् ||
shlokas.transliteration: oṁ ity ekākṣaraṁ brahma vyāharan mām anusmaran | yaḥ prayāti tyajan dehaṁ sa yāti paramāṁ gatim ||
shlokas.translation: Uttering the single syllable Om which is Brahman, remembering Me and leaving the body, such a person attains the supreme destination.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains that one who chants Om the syllable of Brahman while remembering Him and departs the body attains the supreme goal. This verse highlights the power of remembrance at death in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties who think about life's end this verse brings comfort. For example a person who practices chanting Om and remembering the divine daily feels prepared for the final moment with peace. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the syllable Om represents Brahman and remembrance of the divine at death leads to the highest destination. This is a simple yet profound practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes readers eager to incorporate Om in daily life. Practice: chant Om with remembrance of the divine daily so it becomes natural at the end of life.
shlokas.genz: Krishna explains that chanting Om the single syllable of Brahman while remembering Him at the time of leaving the body leads to the supreme goal. This verse highlights the power of remembrance at death in the path to the supreme. For young adults exploring spirituality this verse feels like a simple hack. For example someone who chants Om during meditation and keeps the divine in mind feels connected and ready for anything. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that Om symbolizes Brahman and remembrance at death ensures the highest destination. This is a core practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you eager to make it a habit. Practice: chant Om with remembrance of the divine daily so it flows naturally when needed.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna explains that saying Om the single special word for Brahman while remembering the big friend and leaving the body leads to the best place. This verse shows the power of remembering at the end in the path to the supreme. For children this is like saying a special word and thinking of the big friend before sleep to feel safe. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that Om is Brahman and remembering the divine at the end brings the highest home. This is a simple practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you curious to say Om. Practice: say Om with love for the big friend daily so you feel safe and happy.

shlokas.sanskrit:
सर्वद्वाराणि संयम्य मनो हृदि निरुध्य च |
मूर्ध्न्याधायात्मनः प्राणमास्थितो योगधारणाम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
sarva-dvārāṇi saṁyamya mano hṛdi nirudhya ca
mūrdhny ādhāyātmanaḥ prāṇam āsthito yoga-dhāraṇām
shlokas.translation: At the time of death one should close all the doors of the senses fix the mind in the heart and place the life air at the top of the head while engaged in the practice of yoga.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the process at death closing the senses fixing the mind in the heart placing the life air at the head while in yoga. This verse gives the technique for the final moment in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties thinking about end of life this verse provides guidance. For example someone who practices breath control and focus daily is prepared for the transition. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the yogic method to direct the life force upward at death for the supreme destination. This is part of transcendental knowledge for the final journey. The verse makes readers eager to practice for readiness. Practice: practice breath control and focus to direct energy upward so you are ready for the final moment.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the process at death closing the senses fixing the mind in the heart placing the life air at the head while in yoga. This verse gives the technique for the final moment in the path to the supreme. For young adults thinking about transitions this verse provides guidance. For example someone who practices breath control daily feels prepared for big changes. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the yogic method to direct the life force upward at death for the supreme destination. This is part of transcendental knowledge for the final journey. The verse makes you eager to practice for readiness. Practice: practice breath control and focus to direct energy upward so you are ready for the final moment.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna describes the process at the end closing the senses fixing the mind in the heart placing the life air at the head while in yoga. This verse gives the technique for the final moment in the path to the supreme. For children this is like breathing calmly and thinking of the big friend at bedtime. For example a child who breathes slowly and thinks of the big friend feels safe. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the yogic method to direct energy upward at the end for the supreme home. This is part of transcendental knowledge for the final journey. The verse makes you curious to practice. Practice: practice calm breathing and focus to direct energy upward so you feel safe.

shlokas.sanskrit:
ओमित्येकाक्षरं ब्रह्म व्याहरन्मामनुस्मरन् |
यः प्रयाति त्यजन्देहं स याति परमां गतिम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
om ity ekākṣaraṁ brahma vyāharan mām anusmaran
yaḥ prayāti tyajan dehaṁ sa yāti paramāṁ gatim
shlokas.translation: After uttering the syllable Om the eternal Brahman and remembering Me one who departs the body attains the supreme destination.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna says that one who chants Om the syllable of Brahman and remembers Him at death attains the supreme goal. This verse emphasizes the practice of Om and remembrance. For people in their thirties preparing for life's end this verse is comforting. For example someone who chants Om daily finds it natural at critical times. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that chanting Om and remembering the divine at death leads to the highest destination. This is a simple yet powerful practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes readers eager to incorporate Om. Practice: chant Om with remembrance of the divine daily so it is easy at the end.
shlokas.genz: Krishna says that one who chants Om the syllable of Brahman and remembers Him at death attains the supreme goal. This verse emphasizes the practice of Om and remembrance. For young adults exploring mantras this verse is practical. For example someone who chants Om during meditation finds it natural at key moments. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that chanting Om and remembering the divine at death leads to the highest destination. This is a simple yet powerful practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you eager to incorporate Om. Practice: chant Om with remembrance of the divine daily so it is easy at the end.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna says that one who says Om the special word for Brahman and remembers the big friend at the end attains the best place. This verse emphasizes the practice of Om and remembrance. For children this is like saying a special word and thinking of the big friend before sleep. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that saying Om and remembering the divine at the end leads to the highest home. This is a simple practice in transcendental knowledge. The verse makes you curious to say Om. Practice: say Om with love for the big friend daily so it is easy at bedtime.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अनन्यचेताः सततं यो मां स्मरति नित्यशः |
तस्याहं सुलभः पार्थ नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
ananya-cetāḥ satataṁ yo māṁ smarati nityaśaḥ
tasyāhaṁ sulabhaḥ pārtha nitya-yuktasya yoginaḥ
shlokas.translation: For one who always remembers Me without deviation O Pārtha I am easy to attain for the yogi who is constantly engaged in devotion.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that for one who constantly remembers Him without deviation He is easy to attain. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties with busy lives this verse shows remembrance is accessible. For example someone who keeps the divine in mind during daily tasks finds the supreme close. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that unwavering remembrance makes the divine easily attainable. This is the simple path of devotion. The verse makes readers eager to practice constant remembrance. Practice: keep the divine in mind throughout the day so attainment becomes easy.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that for one who constantly remembers Him without deviation He is easy to attain. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance in the path to the supreme. For young adults with busy lives this verse shows remembrance is accessible. For example someone who keeps higher energy in mind during daily tasks finds the supreme close. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that unwavering remembrance makes the divine easily attainable. This is the simple path of devotion. The verse makes you eager to practice constant remembrance. Practice: keep the divine in mind throughout the day so attainment becomes easy.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna assures that for one who constantly remembers Him without deviation He is easy to attain. This verse emphasizes constant remembrance in the path to the supreme. For children this is like thinking of the big friend all the time making him very close. For example a child who thinks of the big friend during play feels him near. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that unwavering remembrance makes the divine easily attainable. This is the simple path of devotion. The verse makes you curious to practice constant remembrance. Practice: keep the big friend in mind throughout the day so he becomes easy to feel.

shlokas.sanskrit:
मामुपेत्य पुनर्जन्म दुःखालयमशाश्वतम् |
नाप्नुवन्ति महात्मानः संसिद्धिं परमां गताः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
mām upetya punar janma duḥkhālayam aśāśvatam
nāpnuvanti mahātmānāḥ saṁsiddhiṁ paramāṁ gatāḥ
shlokas.translation: After attaining Me the great souls who are yogis in devotion never return to this temporary world full of miseries because they have attained the highest perfection.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna promises that those who attain Him never return to this temporary world of suffering as they have reached the highest perfection. This verse assures liberation. For people in their thirties seeking freedom from cycles this verse brings hope. For example someone who attains divine union feels no fear of rebirth. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that attaining the supreme ends rebirth in the world of misery. This is the goal of the path to the supreme. The verse makes readers eager to attain Him. Practice: strive to attain the divine so you reach the highest perfection and end the cycle.
shlokas.genz: Krishna promises that those who attain Him never return to this temporary world of suffering as they have reached the highest perfection. This verse assures liberation. For young adults seeking freedom this verse brings hope. For example someone who attains divine union feels no fear of the loop. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that attaining the supreme ends rebirth in misery. This is the goal of the path to the supreme. The verse makes you eager to attain Him. Practice: strive to attain the divine so you reach the highest perfection and end the cycle.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna promises that those who attain Him never return to this temporary world of suffering as they have reached the highest perfection. This verse assures liberation. For children this is like reaching the big friend and never having to leave the happy place. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that attaining the supreme ends rebirth in misery. This is the goal of the path to the supreme. The verse makes you curious to attain Him. Practice: strive to attain the divine so you reach the highest perfection and end the cycle.

shlokas.sanskrit:
आब्रह्मभुवनाल्लोकाः पुनरावर्तिनोऽर्जुन |
मामुपेत्य तु कौन्तेय पुनर्जन्म न विद्यते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
ā-brahma-bhuvanāl lokāḥ punar āvartino ’rjuna
mām upetya tu kaunteya punar janma na vidyate
shlokas.translation: From the highest planet in the material world down to the lowest all are places of misery wherein repeated birth and death take place. But one who attains to My abode O son of Kuntī never takes birth again.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna says all material worlds from highest to lowest are places of repeated birth and death but attaining His abode ends rebirth. This verse contrasts material and spiritual realms. For people in their thirties seeking permanence this verse brings hope. For example realizing all worldly achievements are temporary motivates seeking the eternal abode. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that material existence is cyclic misery while the divine abode is permanent freedom. This knowledge inspires transcendence. The verse makes readers eager for the abode. Practice: focus on attaining the divine abode so you end the cycle of birth and death.
shlokas.genz: Krishna says all material worlds from highest to lowest are places of repeated birth and death but attaining His abode ends rebirth. This verse contrasts material and spiritual realms. For young adults seeking permanence this verse brings hope. For example realizing all trends and successes are temporary motivates seeking the eternal. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that material existence is cyclic misery while the divine abode is permanent freedom. This knowledge inspires transcendence. The verse makes you eager for the abode. Practice: focus on attaining the divine abode so you end the cycle of birth and death.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna says all material worlds from highest to lowest are places of repeated birth and death but attaining His abode ends rebirth. This verse contrasts material and spiritual realms. For children this is like knowing all games end but the big friend's home is forever. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that material existence is cyclic misery while the divine abode is permanent freedom. This knowledge inspires transcendence. The verse makes you curious for the abode. Practice: focus on attaining the divine abode so you end the cycle of birth and death.

shlokas.sanskrit:
सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तमहर्यद्ब्रह्मणो विदुः |
रात्रिं युगसहस्रान्तां तेऽहोरात्रविदो जनाः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
sahasra-yuga-paryantam ahar yad brahmaṇo viduḥ
rātriṁ yuga-sahasrāntāṁ te ’horātra-vido janāḥ
shlokas.translation: By human calculation a thousand ages taken together form the duration of Brahma's one day. And such also is the duration of his night.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains that a thousand ages make Brahma's day and a thousand ages make his night. This verse introduces cosmic time scales. For people in their thirties contemplating time this verse puts life in perspective. For example realizing human life is short compared to cosmic cycles motivates spiritual focus. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the vast time of Brahma to show the impermanence of material existence. This knowledge encourages detachment. The verse makes readers eager for more on cosmic cycles. Practice: reflect on the vastness of time so daily life feels temporary and spiritual practice becomes priority.
shlokas.genz: Krishna explains that a thousand ages make Brahma's day and a thousand ages make his night. This verse introduces cosmic time scales. For young adults thinking about time this verse puts life in perspective. For example realizing human life is short compared to cosmic cycles motivates spiritual focus. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the vast time of Brahma to show the impermanence of material existence. This knowledge encourages detachment. The verse makes you eager for more on cosmic cycles. Practice: reflect on the vastness of time so daily life feels temporary and spiritual practice becomes priority.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna explains that a thousand ages make Brahma's day and a thousand ages make his night. This verse introduces cosmic time scales. For children this is like knowing one day for Brahma is very long for us. For example a child who hears that Brahma's day is thousands of years feels the big time. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the vast time of Brahma to show material things are short. This knowledge encourages focus on forever. The verse makes you curious for more. Practice: think of big time so daily play feels short and you focus on lasting happy.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अव्यक्ताद्विभवन्ति भूतानि प्रभवन्त्यहोरात्रे |
रात्र्यागमे प्रलीयन्ते तत्रैवाव्यक्तसंज्ञके ||
shlokas.transliteration:
avyaktād vibhavanti bhūtāni prabhavanty aho-rātre
rātry-āgame pralīyante tatraivāvyakta-saṁjñake
shlokas.translation: At the beginning of Brahma's day all living entities become manifest from the unmanifest state and at the fall of night they are merged into the unmanifest again.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains that at Brahma's daybreak beings emerge from the unmanifest and at night they merge back. This verse describes cosmic cycles. For people in their thirties contemplating cycles this verse puts life in context. For example understanding daily life as part of larger cycles helps accept change. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that creation and dissolution happen in cosmic days and nights. This knowledge reduces attachment to the temporary. The verse makes readers eager for more on the cycles. Practice: see your life as part of larger cycles so you accept changes with wisdom.
shlokas.genz: Krishna explains that at Brahma's daybreak beings emerge from the unmanifest and at night they merge back. This verse describes cosmic cycles. For young adults contemplating cycles this verse puts life in context. For example understanding daily life as part of larger cycles helps accept change. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that creation and dissolution happen in cosmic days and nights. This knowledge reduces attachment to the temporary. The verse makes you eager for more on the cycles. Practice: see your life as part of larger cycles so you accept changes with wisdom.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna explains that at Brahma's daybreak beings emerge from the unmanifest and at night they merge back. This verse describes cosmic cycles. For children this is like the world waking up and sleeping in big days and nights. For example a child who sees the day start and end feels the big cycle. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that creation and dissolution happen in cosmic days and nights. This knowledge reduces attachment to the temporary. The verse makes you curious for more on the cycles. Practice: see your day as part of bigger cycles so you accept changes with ease.

shlokas.sanskrit:
भूतग्रामः स एवायं भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते |
रात्र्यागमेऽवशः पार्थ प्रभवत्यहरागमे ||
shlokas.transliteration:
bhūta-grāmaḥ sa evāyaṁ bhūtvā bhūtvā pralīyate
rātry-āgame ’vaśaḥ pārtha prabhavaty ahar-āgame
shlokas.translation: Again and again when Brahma's night falls the living entities are merged into the unmanifest and when the day arrives they are born again O son of Pṛthā.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains that beings repeatedly emerge at Brahma's daybreak and merge at nightfall helplessly. This verse illustrates the endless cycle. For people in their thirties tired of repetition this verse shows the cycle. For example realizing life patterns repeat until transcendence. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the cyclic birth and dissolution under cosmic time. This knowledge motivates breaking the cycle. The verse makes readers eager for the way out. Practice: reflect on life's repetitions to motivate seeking transcendence.
shlokas.genz: Krishna explains that beings repeatedly emerge at Brahma's daybreak and merge at nightfall helplessly. This verse illustrates the endless cycle. For young adults tired of repetition this verse shows the cycle. For example realizing life patterns repeat until transcendence. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the cyclic birth and dissolution under cosmic time. This knowledge motivates breaking the cycle. The verse makes you eager for the way out. Practice: reflect on life's repetitions to motivate seeking transcendence.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna explains that beings repeatedly emerge at Brahma's daybreak and merge at nightfall helplessly. This verse illustrates the endless cycle. For children this is like the world waking and sleeping again and again. For example a child who sees day and night repeating feels the big cycle. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the cyclic birth and dissolution under cosmic time. This knowledge motivates breaking the cycle. The verse makes you curious for the way out. Practice: reflect on life's repetitions to motivate seeking transcendence.

shlokas.sanskrit:
परस्तस्मात्तु भावोऽन्योऽव्यक्तोऽव्यक्तात्सनातनः |
यः स सर्वेषु भूतेषु नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति ||
shlokas.transliteration:
paras tasmāt tu bhāvo ’nyo ’vyakto ’vyaktāt sanātanaḥ
yaḥ sa sarveṣu bhūteṣu naśyatsu na vinaśyati
shlokas.translation: Yet there is another unmanifest nature which is eternal and is transcendental to this manifested and unmanifested matter. It is supreme and is never annihilated even when the material world is annihilated.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna reveals a higher unmanifest eternal nature beyond the material and even the unmanifest. It remains when everything is destroyed. This verse introduces the supreme reality. For people in their thirties contemplating existence this verse brings the highest perspective. For example understanding that beyond material cycles there is an eternal reality gives purpose. Detailed meaning: Krishna distinguishes the supreme eternal unmanifest from material nature. This knowledge is key to wisdom. The verse makes readers eager to know this supreme. Practice: meditate on the eternal beyond the changing so you anchor in the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna reveals a higher unmanifest eternal nature beyond the material and even the unmanifest. It remains when everything is destroyed. This verse introduces the supreme reality. For young adults contemplating existence this verse brings the highest perspective. For example understanding that beyond material cycles there is an eternal reality gives purpose. Detailed meaning: Krishna distinguishes the supreme eternal unmanifest from material nature. This knowledge is key to wisdom. The verse makes you eager to know this supreme. Practice: meditate on the eternal beyond the changing so you anchor in the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna reveals a higher unmanifest eternal nature beyond the material and even the unmanifest. It remains when everything is destroyed. This verse introduces the supreme reality. For children this is like knowing beyond the changing toys there is a forever happy place. Detailed meaning: Krishna distinguishes the supreme eternal unmanifest from material nature. This knowledge is key to wisdom. The verse makes you curious to know this supreme. Practice: meditate on the eternal beyond the changing so you anchor in the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अव्यक्तोऽक्षर इत्युक्तस्तमाहुः परमां गतिम् |
यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम ||
shlokas.transliteration:
avyakto ’kṣara ity uktas tam āhuḥ paramāṁ gatim
yaṁ prāpya na nivartante tad dhāma paramaṁ mama
shlokas.translation: That which is unmanifest and infallible is called the supreme destination. Those who reach it never return. That is My supreme abode.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the unmanifest eternal realm as the supreme destination. Those who attain it never return to the material world. This verse reveals the highest goal in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties who work toward long term stability this verse brings the vision of permanent peace. For example a professional who dedicates years to ethical leadership and inner growth finally reaches a state where daily pressures no longer bind him and he experiences lasting fulfillment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the supreme abode is beyond manifestation and once reached there is no return to cycles of birth and death. This knowledge motivates striving for the eternal. The verse makes readers eager to aim for this destination. Practice: dedicate your efforts to higher principles daily so your life aligns with the supreme abode and brings permanent freedom.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the unmanifest eternal realm as the supreme destination. Those who attain it never return to the material world. This verse reveals the highest goal in the path to the supreme. For young adults who seek lasting freedom this verse brings the vision of permanent peace. For example a creator who focuses on meaningful work beyond trends finally reaches a state where external validation no longer binds him and he experiences lasting fulfillment. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the supreme abode is beyond manifestation and once reached there is no return to cycles of birth and death. This knowledge motivates striving for the eternal. The verse makes you eager to aim for this destination. Practice: dedicate your efforts to higher principles daily so your life aligns with the supreme abode and brings permanent freedom.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna describes the unmanifest eternal realm as the supreme destination. Those who attain it never return to the material world. This verse reveals the highest goal in the path to the supreme. For children this is like reaching the forever happy home where games never end sadly. For example a child who plays with love for the big friend finally feels safe and free forever. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the supreme abode is beyond manifestation and once reached there is no return to cycles of birth and death. This knowledge motivates striving for the eternal. The verse makes you curious to aim for this destination. Practice: dedicate your play to higher principles daily so your life aligns with the supreme abode and brings permanent freedom.

shlokas.sanskrit:
पुरुषः स परः पार्थ भक्त्या लभ्यस्त्वनन्यया |
यस्यान्तःस्थानि भूतानि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
puruṣaḥ sa paraḥ pārtha bhaktyā labhyas tv ananyayā
yasyāntaḥsthāni bhūtāni yena sarvam idaṁ tatam
shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead who is greater than all is attainable by unalloyed devotion. Although He is present in His abode He is all pervading and everything is situated within Him.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna teaches that the supreme person is attained by exclusive devotion. He is all pervading and everything rests within Him. This verse shows the way to the supreme. For people in their thirties building relationships this verse highlights devotion as the key. For example a leader who offers his work with pure devotion finds closeness to the divine in every responsibility. Detailed meaning: Krishna reveals that the supreme is reached through undivided love and He pervades all existence. This knowledge inspires exclusive focus. The verse makes readers eager to practice devotion. Practice: offer your daily actions with undivided love so you attain the supreme who pervades everything.
shlokas.genz: Krishna teaches that the supreme person is attained by exclusive devotion. He is all pervading and everything rests within Him. This verse shows the way to the supreme. For young adults building connections this verse highlights devotion as the key. For example a friend who shares with pure heart finds closeness to the divine in every interaction. Detailed meaning: Krishna reveals that the supreme is reached through undivided love and He pervades all existence. This knowledge inspires exclusive focus. The verse makes you eager to practice devotion. Practice: offer your daily actions with undivided love so you attain the supreme who pervades everything.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna teaches that the supreme person is attained by exclusive devotion. He is all pervading and everything rests within Him. This verse shows the way to the supreme. For children this is like reaching the big friend through pure love. For example a child who shares with full heart feels the big friend close. Detailed meaning: Krishna reveals that the supreme is reached through undivided love and He pervades all existence. This knowledge inspires exclusive focus. The verse makes you curious to practice devotion. Practice: offer your daily play with undivided love so you attain the supreme who pervades everything.

shlokas.sanskrit:
यत्र काले त्वनावृत्तिमावृत्तिं चैव योगिनः |
प्रयाता यान्ति तं कालं वक्ष्यामि भरतर्षभ ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yatra kāle tv anāvṛttim āvṛttiṁ caiva yoginaḥ
prayātā yānti taṁ kālaṁ vakṣyāmi bharatarṣabha
shlokas.translation: O best of the Bharatas I shall now explain to you the time at which a yogi leaves this world and does not return and the time at which he leaves and returns.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna promises to explain the times when a yogi departs and does not return or returns again. This verse introduces the cosmic timing of death. For people in their thirties contemplating transitions this verse provides guidance. For example a person planning long term goals understands the importance of the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna prepares to describe the auspicious and inauspicious times for leaving the body. This knowledge is part of the path to the supreme. The verse makes readers eager for the details. Practice: live with awareness of the final moment so your choices align with the path to the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna promises to explain the times when a yogi departs and does not return or returns again. This verse introduces the cosmic timing of death. For young adults thinking about big endings this verse provides guidance. For example a person planning life steps understands the importance of the final moment. Detailed meaning: Krishna prepares to describe the auspicious and inauspicious times for leaving the body. This knowledge is part of the path to the supreme. The verse makes you eager for the details. Practice: live with awareness of the final moment so your choices align with the path to the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna promises to explain the times when a yogi departs and does not return or returns again. This verse introduces the cosmic timing of death. For children this is like knowing the right time to end a game and go home happily. Detailed meaning: Krishna prepares to describe the auspicious and inauspicious times for leaving the body. This knowledge is part of the path to the supreme. The verse makes you curious for the details. Practice: live with awareness of the final moment so your choices align with the path to the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अग्निर्ज्योतिरहः शुक्लः षण्मासा उत्तरायणम् |
तत्र प्रयाता गच्छन्ति ब्रह्म ब्रह्मविदो जनाः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
agnir jyotir ahaḥ śuklaḥ ṣaṇmāsā uttarāyaṇam
tatra prayātā gacchanti brahma brahma-vido janāḥ
shlokas.translation: Those who know the Supreme Brahman attain that Supreme by passing away from the world during the influence of fire during the day during the fortnight of the waxing moon or during the six months when the sun travels in the north.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the auspicious times for departure fire day bright fortnight and northern sun path. Those who know Brahman attain it then. This verse details the favorable path in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties planning long term this verse gives cosmic timing. For example a person who lives righteously finds the final moment aligned with higher forces. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to Brahman. This knowledge guides righteous living. The verse makes readers eager to live accordingly. Practice: align your life with righteousness so your departure leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the auspicious times for departure fire day bright fortnight and northern sun path. Those who know Brahman attain it then. This verse details the favorable path in the path to the supreme. For young adults thinking about timing this verse gives cosmic guidance. For example a person who lives righteously finds the final moment aligned with higher forces. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to Brahman. This knowledge guides righteous living. The verse makes you eager to live accordingly. Practice: align your life with righteousness so your departure leads to the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna describes the auspicious times for departure fire day bright fortnight and northern sun path. Those who know Brahman attain it then. This verse details the favorable path in the path to the supreme. For children this is like knowing the right time to go home happily. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to Brahman. This knowledge guides righteous living. The verse makes you curious to live accordingly. Practice: align your life with righteousness so your departure leads to the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
धूमो रात्रिस्तथा कृष्णः षण्मासा दक्षिणायनम् |
तत्र चान्द्रमसं ज्योतिर्योगी प्राप्य निवर्तते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
dhūmo rātris tathā kṛṣṇaḥ ṣaṇmāsā dakṣiṇāyanam
tatra cāndramasaṁ jyotir yogī prāpya nivartate
shlokas.translation: The mystic who passes away from this world during the smoke at night during the waning of the moon or during the six months when the sun passes to the south reaches the moon planet but again returns.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the inauspicious times smoke night dark fortnight and southern sun path. The yogi reaches the moon but returns. This verse contrasts the paths in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties making choices this verse warns of cycles. For example a person who lives without higher awareness may return to material life. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to temporary realms. This knowledge encourages choosing the higher path. The verse makes readers eager to avoid return. Practice: live with awareness of the higher path so you avoid temporary destinations.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the inauspicious times smoke night dark fortnight and southern sun path. The yogi reaches the moon but returns. This verse contrasts the paths in the path to the supreme. For young adults making choices this verse warns of cycles. For example a person who lives without higher awareness may return to material life. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to temporary realms. This knowledge encourages choosing the higher path. The verse makes you eager to avoid return. Practice: live with awareness of the higher path so you avoid temporary destinations.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna describes the inauspicious times smoke night dark fortnight and southern sun path. The yogi reaches the moon but returns. This verse contrasts the paths in the path to the supreme. For children this is like choosing the wrong time to go home and coming back. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains the times when departure leads to temporary realms. This knowledge encourages choosing the higher path. The verse makes you curious to avoid return. Practice: live with awareness of the higher path so you avoid temporary destinations.

shlokas.sanskrit:
शुक्लकृष्णे गती ह्येते जगतः शाश्वते मते |
एकया यात्यनावृत्तिमन्ययावर्तते पुनः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śukla-kṛṣṇe gatī hy ete jagataḥ śāśvate mate
ekayā yāty anāvṛttim anyayāvartate punaḥ
shlokas.translation: According to Vedic opinion there are two ways of passing from this world one in light and one in darkness. When one passes in light he does not come back but when one passes in darkness he returns.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains the two paths light and dark. The path of light leads to no return while the dark path brings return. This verse contrasts the destinations in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties choosing lifestyles this verse guides decisions. For example a person who lives with higher awareness chooses the path of light. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the two cosmic paths determining return or liberation. This knowledge helps choose the higher. The verse makes readers eager to follow the light. Practice: live with higher awareness so you take the path of light and do not return.
shlokas.genz: Krishna explains the two paths light and dark. The path of light leads to no return while the dark path brings return. This verse contrasts the destinations in the path to the supreme. For young adults choosing lifestyles this verse guides decisions. For example a person who lives with higher awareness chooses the path of light. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the two cosmic paths determining return or liberation. This knowledge helps choose the higher. The verse makes you eager to follow the light. Practice: live with higher awareness so you take the path of light and do not return.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna explains the two paths light and dark. The path of light leads to no return while the dark path brings return. This verse contrasts the destinations in the path to the supreme. For children this is like the bright way home or the dark way back. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches the two cosmic paths determining return or liberation. This knowledge helps choose the higher. The verse makes you curious to follow the light. Practice: live with higher awareness so you take the path of light and do not return.

shlokas.sanskrit:
नैते सृती पार्थ जानन् योगी मुह्यति कश्चन |
तस्मात्सर्वेषु कालेषु योगयुक्तो भवार्जुन ||
shlokas.transliteration:
naite sṛtī pārtha jānān yogī muhyati kaścana
tasmāt sarveṣu kāleṣu yoga-yukto bhavārjuna
shlokas.translation: O Arjuna the yogi who knows these two paths never becomes deluded. Therefore be always fixed in yoga O Arjuna.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna advises Arjuna that knowing the two paths prevents delusion and to remain fixed in yoga always. This verse urges constant practice in the path to the supreme. For people in their thirties balancing life this verse calls for steadiness. For example a person who stays fixed in yoga amid changes avoids confusion. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches knowledge of the paths ends delusion and constant yoga is the way. This is the practical conclusion. The verse makes readers eager to stay fixed. Practice: remain fixed in yoga every day so you avoid delusion and reach the supreme.
shlokas.genz: Krishna advises Arjuna that knowing the two paths prevents delusion and to remain fixed in yoga always. This verse urges constant practice in the path to the supreme. For young adults balancing life this verse calls for steadiness. For example a person who stays fixed in yoga amid changes avoids confusion. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches knowledge of the paths ends delusion and constant yoga is the way. This is the practical conclusion. The verse makes you eager to stay fixed. Practice: remain fixed in yoga every day so you avoid delusion and reach the supreme.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna advises Arjuna that knowing the two paths prevents delusion and to remain fixed in yoga always. This verse urges constant practice in the path to the supreme. For children this is like knowing the right way home and staying on it. For example a child who stays on the bright path feels safe. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches knowledge of the paths ends delusion and constant yoga is the way. This is the practical conclusion. The verse makes you curious to stay fixed. Practice: remain fixed in yoga every day so you avoid delusion and reach the supreme.

shlokas.sanskrit:
वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु चैव दानेषु यत्पुण्यफलं प्रदिष्टम् |
अत्येति तत्सर्वमिदं विदित्वा योगी परं स्थानमुपैति चाद्यम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
vedeṣu yajñeṣu tapaḥsu caiva dāneṣu yat puṇya-phalaṁ pradiṣṭam
atyeti tat sarvam idaṁ viditvā yogī paraṁ sthānam upaiti cādyam
shlokas.translation: A person who accepts the result of charity sacrifice austerity and Vedic study as the supreme goal of life attains the supreme destination after death.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that one who knows the results of Vedic study sacrifice austerity and charity as leading to the supreme attains the supreme destination. This verse concludes the chapter. For people in their thirties dedicating efforts this verse gives the reward. For example a person who performs good actions with higher knowledge attains the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that all pious activities when directed to the supreme lead to the highest goal. This is the culmination of the path to the supreme. The verse makes readers eager to apply all practices. Practice: perform your duties with the understanding that they lead to the supreme so you attain the highest destination.
shlokas.genz: Krishna states that one who knows the results of Vedic study sacrifice austerity and charity as leading to the supreme attains the supreme destination. This verse concludes the chapter. For young adults dedicating efforts this verse gives the reward. For example a person who performs good actions with higher knowledge attains the supreme. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that all pious activities when directed to the supreme lead to the highest goal. This is the culmination of the path to the supreme. The verse makes you eager to apply all practices. Practice: perform your duties with the understanding that they lead to the supreme so you attain the highest destination.

This verse concludes the chapter. For children this is like knowing good play and sharing lead to the big happy home. For example a child who does kind things attains the supreme.
